summary: SkiaSharp GL rendering achieves <10 fps (default) or ~40 fps (with OpenGL flag tuning) on macOS compared to ~120 fps for equivalent native Skia C++ code, indicating a significant managed-to-native performance gap in the rendering pipeline.

stepsToReproduce: Run both the native C++ Skia benchmark and the SkiaSharp managed benchmark on macOS
Compare frame rates: native achieves ~120 fps, SkiaSharp achieves <10 fps by default

repoLinks.description: Original benchmark comparing SkiaSharp vs native Skia C++ rendering performance
Fork with Skia downgraded to match SkiaSharp's Skia version, isolating the managed overhead

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SKGLView rendering pipeline and flush patterns have not changed substantially in recent releases. The architectural overhead is inherent to the current view implementation.

analysis.summary: The performance gap between SkiaSharp and native Skia C++ stems from multiple factors in the macOS SKGLView rendering pipeline: a synchronous flush-and-submit pattern that drains the GPU pipeline every frame, 4x MSAA enabled by default in the pixel format, double-flush (canvas + context), and the inherent overhead of the managed-to-native boundary. The fork with downgraded Skia version isolates the managed overhead from Skia version differences.
analysis.rationale: This is clearly a performance bug (type/bug) rather than a feature request because the expectation is that a thin wrapper should approach native performance. The reporter (a project collaborator) provides comprehensive benchmarks with both original and version-matched Skia, giving high confidence in the severity. The area is SkiaSharp.Views because the bottleneck is in the GL view rendering cycle, not in core SkiaSharp APIs.

keySignals.text: C++ version achieves around 120 fps while the managed SkiaSharp code achieves less than 10 fps by default, and up to 40 fps with new OpenGL flags
keySignals.interpretation: 10-12x gap at default, 3x gap with tuning. The 'new OpenGL flags' partially help but a 3x gap remains, suggesting deeper architectural overhead.

keySignals.text: Fork with downgraded Skia (same version as SkiaSharp)
keySignals.interpretation: The version-matched fork isolates the performance gap to the managed wrapper and view infrastructure, ruling out Skia version differences as the sole cause.

codeInvestigation.finding: DrawRect hot path: clears all buffers, conditionally recreates render target on size change, calls user OnPaintSurface, then performs triple flush: canvas.Flush(), context.Flush() (which calls gr_direct_context_flush_and_submit with submit=true), and OpenGLContext.FlushBuffer(). The context.Flush() default is synchronous submit which drains the GPU pipeline every frame.

Pixel format requests 4x MSAA (SampleBuffers=1, Samples=4), 24-bit depth, and 8-bit stencil. This is significantly heavier than typical native Skia benchmarks which often use no MSAA. The 4x MSAA could account for substantial GPU overhead.

GRContext.Flush() calls Flush(true) by default, which invokes gr_direct_context_flush_and_submit(handle, synchronous=false). While not fully synchronous, the submit forces the GPU work to be enqueued immediately rather than batched.

GRContextOptions exposes AvoidStencilBuffers, RuntimeProgramCacheSize, AllowPathMaskCaching, and BufferMapThreshold. These are not used in SKGLView setup - the GRContext is created with default options via GRContext.CreateGl(glInterface) without passing any options.

nextQuestions: What specific 'new OpenGL flags' improved performance from <10fps to ~40fps?
Does the native C++ benchmark use the same MSAA/stencil/depth configuration as SKGLView?
What is the profile breakdown - is the time spent in P/Invoke marshalling, GPU flush, or MSAA?
Would switching to Metal backend on macOS close the gap significantly?
Is the overhead primarily in the view infrastructure or also in individual draw call P/Invoke overhead?

resolution.hypothesis: The performance gap is caused by a combination of heavyweight GL configuration (4x MSAA), synchronous flush-and-submit pattern, and potentially unoptimized GRContext options. The native benchmark likely uses a leaner GL configuration and more efficient frame submission.

proposals.title: Profile and compare GL configurations
proposals.description: Compare the exact GL pixel format, MSAA, depth/stencil, and flush patterns between the native C++ benchmark and SKGLView. The native benchmark may use no MSAA, shallower depth buffer, and async flush. This investigation will identify which factor contributes most to the gap.

proposals.title: Reduce default MSAA and optimize flush pattern
proposals.description: SKGLView requests 4x MSAA by default which is expensive. Consider reducing to no MSAA or making it configurable. Additionally, the triple flush (canvas.Flush + context.Flush with submit + FlushBuffer) could be simplified - canvas.Flush may be redundant when context.Flush follows.

proposals.title: Expose GRContextOptions in view creation
proposals.description: Currently SKGLView creates GRContext with default options. Exposing GRContextOptions (AvoidStencilBuffers, BufferMapThreshold, etc.) would let users tune performance for their workload.

proposals.title: Consider Metal backend on macOS
proposals.description: macOS has deprecated OpenGL in favor of Metal. A Metal-backed view may have lower overhead and better driver optimization than the legacy OpenGL path. This would be a larger effort but may fundamentally resolve the performance gap.

resolution.recommendedReason: Before making code changes, we need to identify exactly which factors contribute most to the 3x remaining gap. The version-matched fork already isolates managed overhead, but we need to know if MSAA, flush pattern, or something else dominates.

actionability.reason: Clear performance bug with comprehensive benchmarks from a project collaborator. Needs profiling to identify root causes before implementing fixes.

actions.description: Apply bug, views, macOS, OpenGL, and performance labels
